description:
Context:
    You are a data-analyst who already has two small CSV files stored under
    /home/user/data.

    1. /home/user/data/employees.csv  
       Columns (comma-separated):  
       • EmployeeID  
       • FirstName  
       • LastName  
       • Department  

    2. /home/user/data/salaries.csv  
       Columns (comma-separated):  
       • EmployeeID  
       • BaseSalary  
       • Bonus  

    Exact current contents of those files are as follows:

    ─ /home/user/data/employees.csv ─
    EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,Department
    101,John,Smith,Engineering
    102,Alice,Johnson,Marketing
    103,Bob,Brown,Sales
    104,Carol,Davis,HR

    ─ /home/user/data/salaries.csv ─
    EmployeeID,BaseSalary,Bonus
    101,75000,5000
    102,68000,4500
    103,72000,4800
    104,65000,4200

    Task:
    Produce a new file called /home/user/output/employee_summary.csv that fulfils
    ALL the requirements below:

    • The directory /home/user/output must exist (create it if necessary).
    • The file must be comma-separated (no spaces around commas).
    • It must contain exactly the following five columns, in order, with a
      single header row:

        EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,BaseSalary,Bonus

    • The data rows must align line-by-line between the two source files so
      that each employee’s salary information matches the correct name.
    • The final file must look EXACTLY like this (including the header row):

      EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,BaseSalary,Bonus
      101,John,Smith,75000,5000
      102,Alice,Johnson,68000,4500
      103,Bob,Brown,72000,4800
      104,Carol,Davis,65000,4200

    Constraints & Hints:
    • Accomplish the task using standard shell utilities only; the
      intent is to practise column manipulation with “cut” and “paste”.
      (A typical solution needs no more than 3–4 commands.)
    • Do NOT hand-edit the files; use command-line tools.
    • Preserve the header row from each file so the new file
      has the correct single header row shown above.
    • Whenever you create the final CSV, also write a small
      log file called /home/user/output/process.log that lists,
      one command per line, the *exact* shell commands you executed
      to build employee_summary.csv.  This file is used for automated
      grading; list only the commands, nothing else.
      Example (illustrative only):

          cut -d',' -f1,2 ...
          paste -d',' ...

      The grader will check that at least one command containing “cut”
      and one containing “paste” appear in that log.

    Verification notes (what the grader will look for):
    1. /home/user/output/employee_summary.csv exists.
    2. It is byte-for-byte identical to the sample block above.
    3. /home/user/output/process.log exists, is plain text,
       contains at least one line with “cut” and one line with “paste”.

    You have no root privileges; stay inside /home/user.
truth:
Initial files present before the agent begins:

    /home/user/data/employees.csv
    --------------------------------
    EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,Department
    101,John,Smith,Engineering
    102,Alice,Johnson,Marketing
    103,Bob,Brown,Sales
    104,Carol,Davis,HR

    /home/user/data/salaries.csv
    --------------------------------
    EmployeeID,BaseSalary,Bonus
    101,75000,5000
    102,68000,4500
    103,72000,4800
    104,65000,4200

    Files/directories that MUST exist after successful completion:

    1. Directory: /home/user/output     (regular dir, 700 or 755 OK)

    2. /home/user/output/employee_summary.csv
       (exact byte sequence shown below)

       EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,BaseSalary,Bonus
       101,John,Smith,75000,5000
       102,Alice,Johnson,68000,4500
       103,Bob,Brown,72000,4800
       104,Carol,Davis,65000,4200
       (Final newline at end of file is optional.)

    3. /home/user/output/process.log
       • Plain text.
       • At least two non-blank lines.
       • One line contains the substring “cut”.
       • One line contains the substring “paste”.
       • Other content is ignored by tests.
